1140 if method == "common": 1141 return round(value, precision) 1142 1143 func = getattr(math, method) 1144 return t.cast(float, func(value * (10**precision)) / (10**precision)) 1145 1146 1147 class _GroupTuple(t.NamedTuple): 1148 grouper: t.Any 1149 list: t.List 1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) 1230 ] 1231 1232 1233 @pass_environment 1234 def sync_do_sum( 1235 environment: "Environment", 1236 iterable: "t.Iterable[V]", 1237 attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None, 1238 start: V = 0, # type: ignore 1239 ) -> V:

380	    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
381	    sorts on different attributes and ordering.
382	
383	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
384	
385	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
386	            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
387	            ...
388	        {% endfor %}
389	
390	    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
391	    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.
392	
393	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
394	
395	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
396	            ...
397	        {% endfor %}
398	
399	    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
400	        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of
401	        attributes, e.g. ``"age,name"``.
402	
403	    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
404	       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.
405	    """
406	    key_func = make_multi_attrgetter(
407	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
408	    )
409	    return sorted(value, key=key_func, reverse=reverse)

412	@pass_environment
413	def do_unique(
414	    environment: "Environment",
415	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
416	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
417	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
418	) -> "t.Iterator[V]":
419	    """Returns a list of unique items from the given iterable.
420	
421	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
422	
423	        {{ ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar', 'FooBar']|unique|list }}
424	            -> ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar']
425	
426	    The unique items are yielded in the same order as their first occurrence in
427	    the iterable passed to the filter.
428	
429	    :param case_sensitive: Treat upper and lower case strings as distinct.
430	    :param attribute: Filter objects with unique values for this attribute.
431	    """
432	    getter = make_attrgetter(
433	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
434	    )
435	    seen = set()
436	
437	    for item in value:
438	        key = getter(item)
439	
440	        if key not in seen:
441	            seen.add(key)
442	            yield item
443	
444	
445	def _min_or_max(
446	    environment: "Environment",
447	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
448	    func: "t.Callable[..., V]",
449	    case_sensitive: bool,
450	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]],
451	) -> "t.Union[V, Undefined]":
452	    it = iter(value)
453	
454	    try:
455	        first = next(it)
456	    except StopIteration:
457	        return environment.undefined("No aggregated item, sequence was empty.")
458	
459	    key_func = make_attrgetter(
460	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
461	    )
462	    return func(chain([first], it), key=key_func)
463

474	    .. sourcecode:: jinja

476	        {{ [1, 2, 3]|min }}
477	            -> 1
478	
479	    :param case_sensitive: Treat upper and lower case strings as distinct.

content.text: Good, `make_attrgetter` supports both `postprocess` and `default`. Now let's implement the change.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will use the case of the first item encountered in each group. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together, under the "CA" key. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat case differences as distinct groups. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default, matching other filters that deal with strings. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the # case-insensitive key. output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ) out = [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ] if not case_sensitive: # Return the real key from the first value instead of the # case-insensitive key. output_expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [_GroupTuple(output_expr(values[0]), values) for _, values in out] return out

content.text: Now let's verify with a quick test.

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment
env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby('city') %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute='name')|join(', ') }}
{% endfor %}''')

class U:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.city = city

users = [U('a', 'CA'), U('b', 'NY'), U('c', 'ca')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
print('---case sensitive---')
tmpl2 = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby('city', case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute='name')|join(', ') }}
{% endfor %}''')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: That's actually correct PEP8 (2 blank lines between top-level defs) — the diff just displayed it oddly because the original had one blank line before the decorator and I added one more before `return out`. It's fine, matches style elsewhere. The patch is finalized.

All 163 tests pass and `patch.txt` is written. I've:

1. Coordinated with agent2 via the handshake protocol — we both needed to touch `groupby()` in the same file/function, so we agreed on a sequential edit order and a shared final signature (`case_sensitive` before their `with_counts` param), then I sent DONE with details of exactly what changed so they can layer on top cleanly.
2. Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, using `ignore_case` as the postprocess on the attrgetter when `case_sensitive=False`, then remapping each group's key back to the original-case value of the first item in that group (so "CA" and "ca" merge under "CA").
3. Verified behavior manually (case-insensitive groups merge; `case_sensitive=true` keeps them separate) and confirmed the full filter test suite still passes.

result:
All 163 tests pass and `patch.txt` is written. I've:

1. Coordinated with agent2 via the handshake protocol — we both needed to touch `groupby()` in the same file/function, so we agreed on a sequential edit order and a shared final signature (`case_sensitive` before their `with_counts` param), then I sent DONE with details of exactly what changed so they can layer on top cleanly.
2. Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`, using `ignore_case` as the postprocess on the attrgetter when `case_sensitive=False`, then remapping each group's key back to the original-case value of the first item in that group (so "CA" and "ca" merge under "CA").
3. Verified behavior manually (case-insensitive groups merge; `case_sensitive=true` keeps them separate) and confirmed the full filter test suite still passes.
